reasoning: The border closures between Benin and Nigeria's Kwara State began on May 7, 2025, due to severe security concerns, including arms smuggling and the emergence of a new terror group ("Mahmuda"). Rather than showing signs of rapid resolution, Benin's response has escalated throughout May, with closures extending to Lagos (May 10) and Ogun (May 21). The physical nature of the blockade (involving heavy stones) and the multi-ministry coordination suggest this is a formalized, non-temporary policy. Historically, border closures in West Africa driven by security or trade disputes (such as Nigeria's own 2019 border closures) generally take many months or even years to resolve. With the resolution date of July 1, 2025, only one month away and no current evidence of breakthrough bilateral diplomacy, the chances of the border being credibly reported as reopened before the deadline are extremely low. I estimate a 5% probability out of an abundance of caution for unforeseen sudden diplomatic interventions.
